title: DOM injection prevention

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for DOM injection prevention; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to DOM injection prevention
sources.claim: DOM XSS occurs when untrusted data reaches an HTML, URL, script, or style execution sink without context-appropriate handling.

sources.claim: DOM XSS occurs when untrusted data reaches an HTML, URL, script, or style execution sink without context-appropriate handling.

canonicalExplanation.summary: DOM XSS occurs when untrusted data reaches an HTML, URL, script, or style execution sink without context-appropriate handling.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A comment is assigned with textContent rather than concatenated into innerHTML. Your goal is to identify and repair DOM XSS sinks using safe text or constrained APIs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: DOM, events and forms; Promises, async work and HTTP; Query and update DOM; Fetch and HTTP responses. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: DOM XSS occurs when untrusted data reaches an HTML, URL, script, or style execution sink without context-appropriate handling. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Escaping differs by context, sanitizers need policy and updates, and trusted types reduce but do not replace design. This draft therefore rejects: Replacing angle brackets is enough to make untrusted text safe in every DOM sink.

includes: Keep untrusted text out of executable HTML, URL and event-handler contexts.
Identify and repair DOM XSS sinks using safe text or constrained APIs.
excludes: Escaping differs by context, sanitizers need policy and updates, and trusted types reduce but do not replace design.

outcomes: Identify and repair DOM XSS sinks using safe text or constrained APIs.

examples.title: DOM injection prevention: concrete case
examples.situation: A comment is assigned with textContent rather than concatenated into innerHTML.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A comment is assigned with textContent rather than concatenated into innerHTML.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Keep untrusted data in textContent or safe typed APIs and avoid parsing it into executable markup.
examples.result: The result follows because Keep untrusted data in textContent or safe typed APIs and avoid parsing it into executable markup.

examples.title: DOM injection prevention: boundary case
examples.situation: Escaping differs by context, sanitizers need policy and updates, and trusted types reduce but do not replace design.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Escaping differs by context, sanitizers need policy and updates, and trusted types reduce but do not replace design.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Replacing angle brackets is enough to make untrusted text safe in every DOM sink.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Keep untrusted data in textContent or safe typed APIs and avoid parsing it into executable markup.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model DOM injection prevention, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for DOM injection prevention before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
